feed.title: NFC WPT with Energy Harvesting - Isa Alkhalifa

entry.title: System’s Components
entry.content:
• Add-On Board, Temp&Hum 9 Click Board, SHTC3 Temperature/Humidity Sensor, MikroBUS
  • Hydro Probe Click Board
  • Development Board, STM32L031K6 MCU, On-Board Debugger, Arduino Compatible
  • Dynamic NFC/RFID tag IC expansion board based on ST25DV04K for STM32 Nucleo
entry.summary: #customers { font-family: Arial, Helvetica, sans-serif; border-collapse: collapse; width: 100%; }

entry.title: Research
entry.content:
Following a table that shows the papers read. For each paper there is a link to the source and the takeaways that I took from it about the potential scope/contribution to my project.

This post will be updated frequently as I find and read more relevant papers.

Papers Read

Title Potential scope/idea for my project
Near-Field Wireless Power Transfer for 6G Internet of Everything Mobile Networks: Opportunities and Challenges Read
NFC Sensors Based on Energy Harvesting for IoT Applications Read
Wireless Power Transfer Using Near-Field Communication for Mobile Devices Read
Wireless charging using NFC technology Read
A Battery-Less NFC Sensor Transponder for Museum Artefact Monitoring Read

 

Near-Field Wireless Power Transfer for 6G Internet of Everything Mobile Networks: Opportunities and Challenges

The paper provides an overview of the opportunities and challenges that arise from radiating near-field WPT. With the transition of 6G systems to mmWave frequencies combined with the use of large-scale antennas, future WPT devices are likely to operate in the radiating near-field.

A limitation of charging wireless devices using NFC WPT is given by the very small distance required between the power source and the charging devices.

6G wireless technologies will utilize large scale antenna arrays to communicate with user devices at the mmWave and sub-THz bands. Therefore, 6G-based IoE devices are expected to transmit and receive EM signals while operating in the radiative near-field.

In such regimes, devices located at distances ranging from a few centimeters to several tens of meters reside in the radiating near-field regime

The fact that IoE devices are likely to receive signals in the near- field regime combined with the potential beam focusing capabilities arising in such domains moti- vate the exploration of radiating near-field WPT for 6G IoE networks.

The expected operation of 6G networks, which will utilize large antenna arrays and high-frequency signals, gives rise to several implications for radiating WPT systems. The short wavelengths of high-frequency signals facilitates packing a large number of antenna elements into a given area, forming a large antenna array of limited physical aperture.

The antenna gain stemming from using large antenna arrays has the potential of compensating for the attenuation of high-frequency signal propagation over distance. The paper demonstrates that despite the WPT for NFC requires a short distance between transmitter and receiver, there are techniques that allow to compensate this issue.

Given that the 6G networks are going to use arrays of antennas, the paper shows how to take advantage of this for increasing the distance (using the beam steering) in which a transmitter can transfer power to receivers using NFC.

 

NFC Sensors Based on Energy Harvesting for IoT Applications

This paper describes how to build a NFC sensors battery less sensors system. The system is powered by harvesting energy from a the electromagnetic induction between 2 loops antennas.

There is a comparison of RFID technologies, which helps in understanding how they can be used in real applications

It shows an example of how the NFC communication between the Transmitter/Reader works and a generic model of the Transmitter and Receiver

NFC IC block diagram


It provides also a list of existing NFC integrated circuits that include energy harvesting, and the amount of current/volt they can provide to feed sensors.

Commercial NFC ICs with energy harvesting

Then there are some advices about how to design and build a NFC reader and Tag. Particular attention has been dedicated about the NFC Tag

When designing an NFC Tag it is important to consider the environment where the tag will work, the application (as it will limit the area for the antenna) and the desired range/. It is a good idea to cover it with a sheet of ferrite for example if the tag will work close to some metal, as it isolates the traces of metal and so it will work better.

Finally it shows some NFC-based sensors system, and how they can be used for building battery-less seonsors system

Battery-less NFC sensor system

This paper was really helpful because provides information about the NFC ICs with energy harvesting present in the market, a workbench comparing their performance and also some suggested usages. It shows the raising interest in the IoT community about building battery-less sensor systems and how the current technology is able to provide enough energy for powering them.

 

Wireless Power Transfer Using Near-Field Communication for Mobile Devices

The paper shows how to create a prototype for transferring power using NFC and harvest the energy for charging a Li-on battery. The work also provides an analysis of the system, with the aim to find the condition when there is high efficiency in power transferred.

It provides also an example of a schematic of for this system, which could be useful when designing my system:

 

Wireless charging using NFC technology

This article talks about the applications where NFC can be used for charging devices using WPT and how to increase the efficiency of the power transmission.

The power that can be provided by NFC charging protocol is lower than the one provided by Qi:


NFC Wireless Charging power class vs Qi power class


As the power class are different, the products targeted by these two technologies are not the same.

Then the article shows how to increase the power transfer efficiency.

The first way is to use the same antenna size for both the transmitter and the receiver. As the target are wereables, the size of the antenna must be small.

Impact of the antennas size on efficiency

The space between the transmitter antenna and the receiver antenna also has an impact of the efficiency.

 

A Battery-Less NFC Sensor Transponder for Museum Artefact Monitoring

The paper present a battery-less NFC low-cost solution for monitoring Cultural Heritage objects. These artefacts are valuable objects housed by museums and are often stored in uncontrolled climatic (temperature/humidity) conditions, which may lead to degradation of them.

One critical disadvantage nowadays of monitoring the storage conditions in these systems is due to the necessity of opening the box when analysis are needed. An NFC temperature and humidity sensor system would allow to monitor them without opening the boxes that contain these items.

The proposed solution is very cheap, as it cost 5 Euro and last around 5 years:

The solution consists on an RF interface and a energy harvesting circuitry, which will connect to the loop antenna. The energy harvester will harvest energy from the magnetic field of an NFC Transmitter, with the help of an internal rectifier and capacitor.

The harvested voltage will further be regulated using a low dropout regulator and will be used to power up an ultra power MCU and the sensors.

The main components of the system are:

  • l031k6 MCU
  • MIKROE-3331 Add-On Board, Temp&Hum 9 Click Board, SHTC3 Temperature/Humidity Sensor, MikroBUS
  • Dynamic NFC/RFID tag IC expansion board based on ST25DV04K for STM32 Nucleo

NFC sensor prototype

The prototype shows that the harvested power from a smartphone with NFC is enough to run the system without a battery.

The article was really interesting because demonstrates that it is possible to power ultra low power sensor systems using NFC WPT, which is the main purpose of my project.
entry.summary: #customers { font-family: Arial, Helvetica, sans-serif; border-collapse: collapse; width: 100%; }

entry.title: Goal, Objective, Tasks
entry.content:
Goal

A battery less sensors system to use in agriculture.

Objective

System able to measure the PH and moisture of the soil using sensors that are powered by Wireless Power Transfer though Near Field Communication.

Tasks

  • Design and Implement an NFC TAG with energy harvester
  • implement a circuit with a low power micro controller and PH and moisture sensors
  • assemble components
  • coding microcontroller
  • send data using NFC to an NFC reader
  • testing
entry.summary: Goal A battery less sensors system to use in agriculture.

entry.title: Welcome to my Major Project Blog

link.title: Welcome to my Major Project Blog

entry.content:
Aim

The aim of this project is to use Near Field Communication for transmitting wireless power (WPT) from a NFC transmitter (reader) to a receiver (tag). The receiver harvests the energy and use it for providing power to a sensors system.

Objectives

  1. Use WPT in Near field communications
  2. Energy Harvesting
  3. Green IoT (Create a battery less system)
  4. System works on short range distance with low cost sensors
  5. Monitor the PH and the moisture of the soil
  6. Data transmission through NFC

Block Diagram

I will build a system that allow to measure and monitor the PH and the moisture of the soil through NFC. This can be useful for personal use at home or for monitoring plants in farms or greenhouses.

The system will be composed by:

  • NFC Reader (a smarthphone with NFC support)
  • Dynamic NFC Tag with Energy Harvester (ie M24LR) and Antenna
  • Low power microcontroller (ie ATTiny85)
  • PH and soil moisture sensors
entry.summary: Aim The aim of this project is to use Near Field Communication for transmitting wireless power (WPT) from a NFC transmitter (reader) to a receiver (tag). The receiver harvests the energy and use it for providing power to a sensors system.
